# The Car You Stop Owning

prev_id: null

## Context

Americans do not just use cars. They finance them, insure them, trade them in,
repair them, park them, register them, borrow against them, build garages for them,
and organize daily life around having one nearby.

Tesla-scale autonomy puts pressure on that ownership pattern. Robotaxi service is
already live in limited Texas areas. Supervised FSD is spreading through owned Tesla
vehicles in multiple countries, while still requiring an attentive driver. The
combined signal is not just that rides may get cheaper. It is that one company is
trying to make autonomy part of a vehicle platform that can be sold, subscribed to,
updated, and possibly used as a service asset.

The U.S. ownership stack is huge. Auto-loan balances were about $1.69 trillion in
Q1 2026. Franchised light-vehicle dealers sold 16.2 million light-duty vehicles in
2025, generated more than $1.3 trillion in sales, and wrote more than 276 million
repair orders. Dealerships are not only showrooms; they are finance desks, trade-in
funnels, local employers, service businesses, warranty channels, political actors,
and beneficiaries of state franchise laws.

The private car also has a utilization problem. It is expensive and sits unused for
most of the day. That made sense when the alternative was waiting, walking, taking
transit that did not match the trip, or paying another human to drive. If a reliable
autonomous ride is always near, the household car has to justify itself differently.

There are at least three live possibilities:

- households keep owning cars because autonomy makes the owned car more useful
- households own fewer cars because cheap service covers ordinary trips
- ownership mutates because a household vehicle can work as a paid robotaxi when
  the owner is not using it

Those paths hit different institutions. A pure service model pressures retail unit
sales, used-car residuals, auto lending, parking, fuel retail, and household
insurance. A household robotaxi model may preserve financing but change what is
being financed: not a depreciating convenience asset, but an income-producing
machine with different risk, wear, tax, repair, and liability questions.

The visible story is again too small. People can understand cheaper rides and driver
job loss. Far fewer are asking what happens to the car as the default household
asset.

### Decision Point

You are looking at the ownership economy before the market settles.

Which parts depend on households buying and maintaining privately used vehicles, and
which parts can survive if mobility shifts toward fleets, subscriptions, or
income-producing owned cars?

## Synopsis

American daily life is organized around owning a car (financing, insurance, repair, parking); the case examines how autonomy pressures that whole ownership pattern.

agenomes.label: Blindside
agenomes.prompt: Look for the non-obvious actor, liability shift, or second-order failure mode.
agenomes.decompositionPolicy: Invert the stated problem and search for the neglected stakeholder.

agenomes.label: Breakout
agenomes.prompt: Escape local framing and import a mechanism from a more useful neighboring domain.
agenomes.decompositionPolicy: Map the case onto a neighboring system with similar pressure geometry.

agenomes.label: Breakthrough
agenomes.prompt: Synthesize a sharp intervention that changes the payoff landscape.
agenomes.decompositionPolicy: Prefer a small mechanism with a large behavioral or economic effect.

agenomes.label: Constraint Injection
agenomes.prompt: Find the hidden operational, legal, or economic constraint that changes the solution.
agenomes.decompositionPolicy: Name the binding constraint before proposing any intervention.

agenomes.label: First Principles
agenomes.prompt: Reduce the case to primitives, then rebuild the mechanism from those primitives.
agenomes.decompositionPolicy: Separate demand, incentives, constraints, and feedback loops.

agenomes.label: Polymath
agenomes.prompt: Combine evidence from multiple domains into one coherent candidate.
agenomes.decompositionPolicy: Fuse analogies only after naming their shared causal structure.

agenomes.label: Skeptic
agenomes.prompt: Attack the leading explanation and produce the strongest falsifiable alternative.
agenomes.decompositionPolicy: Start from the most likely way the candidate could be wrong.

agenomes.label: Fusion: First Principles / Blindside
agenomes.prompt: Fuse the strongest mechanism from First Principles with the constraint pressure from Blindside.
agenomes.decompositionPolicy: Preserve parent mechanisms separately, test compatibility, then synthesize only the traits that survive critic pressure.

controlBaseline.title: The Ownership Stack, Not the Ride
controlBaseline.summary: The load-bearing problem is not whether robotaxi miles get cheaper. It is that Tesla-scale autonomy turns the privately owned vehicle into a platform node whose OEM can absorb insurance settlement, supervised-mile subscription revenue, and eventual fleet dispatch—collapsing the multi-party ownership economy (lender, insurer, adjuster, dealer, household) into a single vertically integrated counterparty while household demand is already fracturing under payment stress.
controlBaseline.mechanism: Separate demand from delivery: household car demand is weakening on payment refusal and utilization math, while Tesla simultaneously sells supervised autonomy into owned vehicles and operates a limited robotaxi fleet in Texas. The binding constraint is not fleet scale but platform capture—telematics plus OEM direct settlement disintermediates the adjuster layer, and supervised FSD keeps the car in the driveway while harvesting miles toward eventual service conversion. The neglected stakeholder is every intermediary whose margin assumes the human driver remains the legal and economic subject of the vehicle.
controlBaseline.claimedDelta: Moves the problem from 'will Americans switch to robotaxis?' to 'which layers of the car-ownership stack break first when the OEM becomes insurer, claims authority, and fleet operator on the same VIN'—grounded in live but limited robotaxi deployment, operational friction in Texas, and telematics-driven claims disintermediation rather than hypothetical full autonomy adoption.

candidates.title: Americans own collateral stacks, not trips
candidates.summary: The stated problem treats car use as the behavior to explain, but the durable pattern is a bundled balance-sheet object: secured debt, insurable collateral, registrable asset, and spatial anchor. Autonomy vendors optimize per-mile trip cost; households optimize monthly obligation, equity extraction, and life-layout fit. Until those layers decouple, cheaper rides do not unwind ownership.
candidates.mechanism: Separate demand (mobility minutes), incentives (loan approval, insurance pricing, trade-in cycles), constraints (parking geometry, commute topology, credit access), and feedback loops (depreciation → underwater loans → longer terms → higher payments → refusal to exit). Trip substitution via robotaxi only attacks the demand layer; finance, insurance, registration, and garage-adjacent lifestyle remain on the collateral loop.
candidates.claimedDelta: Reframes autonomy pressure from a technology race to a collateral-unbundling problem: the real cause of persistent ownership is the integrated capital-and-compliance bundle, not preference for driving.

candidates.title: The neglected owner is the car-dependency industry
candidates.summary: Invert the consumer-centric frame: Americans keep financing and insuring cars because a distributed ownership-maintenance economy—dealers, lenders, insurers, municipalities, repair networks, parking landlords—prices and regulates life around one car nearby. Tesla-scale autonomy threatens that rent chain more than it threatens the driver habit.
candidates.mechanism: Search for the stakeholder whose revenue disappears if ownership thins. Registration fees, property-tax assumptions on garages, dealer F&I spreads, insurer float, and body-shop throughput all assume VIN permanence. Robotaxi at 59 vehicles is small, but OEM telematics plus direct settlement pilots signal platform capture of claims and lifecycle data—disintermediating adjusters first, then weakening the insurer-dealer adjacency that sustains the ownership ritual.
candidates.claimedDelta: Real cause shifts from cultural car love to institutional lock-in: ownership persists because adjacent institutions still earn on every financed, insured, parked, and repaired VIN.

candidates.title: Cars follow the datacenter capex-to-opex fracture
candidates.summary: Map the case onto enterprise infrastructure: organizations kept buying servers long after cloud proved cheaper because capex, depreciation schedules, and vendor maintenance contracts lagged the new unit economics. American car life exhibits the same fracture—mobility opex is arriving while household capex (loan, garage, insurance stack) remains legally and spatially installed.
candidates.mechanism: Shared pressure geometry: (1) high upfront asset, (2) multi-year depreciation contract, (3) specialized maintenance priesthood, (4) switching cost embedded in physical footprint. Supervised FSD and limited robotaxi mirror early cloud—usable in pockets, not yet credible as full substitute. The lagging variable is not technology capability but contract and footprint unwinding.
candidates.claimedDelta: Predicts ownership decay as a staged migration—finance and parking commitments outlive functional trip substitution—rather than a sudden autonomy flip.

candidates.title: One subscription retires five ownership chores
candidates.summary: A small mechanism—VIN- or account-locked mobility subscription that rolls payment, insurance, registration, maintenance, and trade-in into a single usage meter—could collapse the behavioral surface area that makes car ownership feel mandatory even when trips are partially automatable.
candidates.mechanism: High-leverage intervention targets the paperwork bundle, not the powertrain. When the platform owns depreciation risk and bills per mile/month, the household sheds insure-register-repair-trade-in decisions without waiting for full robotaxi density. FSD supervised mode becomes retention feature inside the bundle; robotaxi becomes overflow capacity. Payment refusal becomes the forcing function that makes bundling commercially attractive.
candidates.claimedDelta: Ownership unwinds when a single contract substitutes the chore cluster; autonomy accelerates the bundle only if it is sold as subscription-native, not as an owned-vehicle feature alone.

candidates.title: Seven-year loans meet immovable parking
candidates.summary: Name the binding constraint before any autonomy forecast: amortization length plus residential parking geometry. Americans organize daily life around a car nearby because the driveway, street permit, garage, and commute graph were chosen under ownership assumptions that outlast any robotaxi headline.
candidates.mechanism: Binding constraint = simultaneous (a) secured multi-year debt on a depreciating asset and (b) fixed spatial slot for that asset. Robotaxi reduces marginal trip cost but does not free the parking slot or erase negative equity. Until loan term, housing location, or parking policy changes, households keep insuring and registering a car they barely drive—payment stress is the visible symptom; spatial-financial lock is the cause.
candidates.claimedDelta: Explains why limited robotaxi rollout and operational friction barely dent ownership: the constraint is balance-sheet and land-use, not ride availability.

candidates.title: The car is a mobile mortgaged lifestyle anchor
candidates.summary: Fuse only after naming shared causal structure with homeownership: illiquid collateral tied to daily routine, social status, and emergency liquidity. Americans do not merely drive; they anchor time, credit, and identity to a titled asset that must stay insured, registered, and within reach.
candidates.mechanism: Shared structure across domains: collateralized commitment → sunk spatial choice → insurance/regulatory compliance → identity signaling → inability to exit without simultaneous lifestyle rewrite. Autonomy platforms attack trip execution; they do not automatically replace equity access (refi against car), teen mobility independence, or suburban distance budgets. Supervised FSD extends anchor life; it does not dissolve the secured-lifestyle loop.
candidates.claimedDelta: Real cause is the secured-lifestyle anchor, not transportation preference—explaining why financing and insuring persist even as utilization could fall.

candidates.title: Payment mutiny is not an autonomy vote
candidates.summary: Start from the most likely error: treating payment refusal and Tesla autonomy as one causal story. Americans resisting insane car payments reflects credit-cycle stress and loan-term bloat; 59 robotaxis in three Texas cities and troubled early operations do not yet offer a reliable substitute for the owned-car chore bundle.
candidates.mechanism: Failure mode for the autonomy-disruption narrative: conflate affordability crisis with mode shift. Supervised FSD still requires an attentive driver in owned vehicles—reinforcing ownership. Robotaxi remains patchy and operationally brittle, so households cannot offload finance-insure-park-register chains at scale. The real near-term cause of ownership stress is payment arithmetic; the real near-term cause of ownership persistence is absent credible substitute for the whole bundle.
candidates.claimedDelta: Splits two phenomena—credit pain versus platform shift—preventing false confidence that robotaxi headlines explain why Americans still finance, insure, and garage cars.

criticVerdicts.pressure: The frame is anchored in the case's observable stack—auto credit, dealer finance desks, repair orders, parking, insurance—but it inventories institutions without naming which public signal moves first.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Pick one leading indicator per stack layer (loan delinquency, used-residual auction prints, floorplan days, insured-mile pricing) and state the threshold that would count as unwind, not cyclical stress.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Ownership stack is already the case's organizing noun; the clean frame restates the seed instead of recovering a sharper load-bearing problem behind cheaper rides or Tesla platform economics.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Recover the premise the case hides: autonomy removes the human-driver reason for household ownership, and expose the service-inventory versus owned-yield fork as the problem's decisive constraint.

criticVerdicts.pressure: The mechanism maps parallel stack layers under autonomy pressure but treats finance, dealers, parking, and insurance as symmetric casualties, blurring three live paths the case names.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Split the causal chain by path—pure fleet service, fewer owned cars, owned robotaxi yield—and name which stack layer breaks first under each before collapsing them into one unwind story.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Strong because it maps directly onto the stated finance-insure-park-register bundle and separates the mobility service from the collateral stack Americans actually buy.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: List which bundle line items unwind first when miles shift to fleet inventory versus owned-hybrid yield.

criticVerdicts.pressure: The collateral-bundle decomposition is sharper than generic robotaxi economics, though residual-value framing is nearby.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Keep the pure-mobility versus owned-collateral fork explicit so it does not collapse into ordinary auto-credit analysis.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Mechanism is load-bearing: autonomy removes the driver reason for holding the bundle, so each adjacent business reprices against utilization not idle ownership.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: State the falsifier threshold on household replacement rates if fleet miles stay marginal.

criticVerdicts.pressure: The repair-insure-dealer-DMV-parking ecosystem is observable and already named in the problem, giving the blindside a concrete dependent map.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Rank the first three maintenance-economy actors whose revenue breaks before new-car sales fall.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Shifts attention from Tesla platform headlines to the neglected stakeholders whose margins require perpetual private ownership churn.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Name one counterparty outside insurance and dealers that is not already implied by the case text.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Mechanism is credible—fleet utilization starves the maintenance economy—but needs a leading indicator rather than a sector list.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Tie the economy map to a single metric such as service-bay hours per registered private vehicle.

criticVerdicts.pressure: The capex-to-opex analogy is intelligible but stretches because vehicles are depreciating consumer assets with title, insurance, and locality constraints unlike fungible compute.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Specify which datacenter migration step has a public filing or utilization dataset analog in auto fleets.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Useful frame escape from car-nerd discourse into infrastructure repricing, even if the analogy is imperfect.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Map one non-obvious second-order market unlocked when mobility becomes metered opex instead of owned capex.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Mechanism gestures at utilization economics but does not yet show the causal link from cloud-style substitution to household garage abandonment.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Choose the first household budget line that flips from capex to subscription and identify its break-even mile price.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Grounded in the case signal that one company is bundling autonomy into a sellable and subscribable vehicle platform with identifiable VIN-level telemetry.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Cite the observable subscription or utilization fields that would prove VIN-level metering is pricing behavior, not marketing.

criticVerdicts.pressure: More specific than generic fleet thesis and captures the platform-lock dimension implied by supervised FSD on owned cars.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Separate the meter from ordinary telematics insurance so the intervention stays distinct.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Mechanism is tight: VIN-locked metering turns each owned car into a revenue node and makes pure-service exit costly, which directly pressures the ownership bundle.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: State when meter revenue exceeds loan payment for a median owner and what that implies for trade-in timing.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Parking and garage commitments are real household constraints, though the seven-year horizon needs empirical support.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Anchor the seven-year lock to a dataset on lease terms, HOA garaging rules, or commute-housing pairing.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Useful constraint injection, but parking lock-in is a known friction in urban mobility transitions.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Show why this constraint binds harder under owned supervised FSD than under ordinary ride-hail availability.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Mechanism explains delayed ownership unwind through sunk housing-parking commitments, but needs a geographic segmentation.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Name the first metro where parking lock breaks before vehicle sales decline and define the leading indicator.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Secured-lending and lifestyle-collateral patterns are well documented, and auto title lending plus home-garage pairing gives the analogy footing.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Separate title-loan exposure from ordinary auto ABS and tie each to a public data source.

criticVerdicts.pressure: The lifestyle-anchor frame imports a useful cross-domain lens without repeating the robotaxi headline.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Identify the shared causal structure with mortgage or HELOC anchoring before keeping the analogy.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Mechanism links ownership persistence to collateralized daily-life organization, but the unwind path is still broad.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: State which secured obligation unwinds first when autonomous miles become a service line item.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Payment stress and delinquency are observable and can move independently of autonomy deployment curves.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Define the auto-credit indicators that would falsify an autonomy-driven ownership unwind.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Valuable skeptic lane that challenges causal ordering, though it risks sounding like generic macro auto-credit commentary.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Keep the decoupling claim tied to the Tesla platform fork rather than broad household leverage alone.

criticVerdicts.pressure: Mechanism attacks the thesis more than it explains the ownership bundle unwind the case asks to solve.
criticVerdicts.revisionMandate: Convert the skepticism into a concrete sorting rule for when to weight credit stress over autonomy adoption.

axes.reasoning: The collateral-stack bundle and demand-layer-only critique sharpen familiar mobility-as-a-service debates, but institutional lock-in around financed VINs is already well explored in urban policy and auto-finance literature.

It ties a coherent mechanism to observable facts—longer loan terms, underwater depreciation loops, dealer F&I spreads, registration revenue, robotaxi pilots, and OEM telematics in claims—rather than resting on cultural caricature alone.

Cohort trends in ownership versus per-mile mobility cost and usage-based insurance adoption could test the thesis, but separating collateral bundling from land-use constraints and stated preference would need cleaner quasi-experiments than the frame alone supplies.

As a reframing move it is compact and high-leverage, redirecting inquiry from fleet autonomy economics toward finance-insurance-registration decoupling without requiring a large new empirical apparatus.
It directly addresses why autonomy and cheaper trips may fail to thin ownership in the U.S. right now, when robotaxi rollouts, FSD subscriptions, and auto-loan stress are simultaneously live.

{
  "candidates": [
    {
      "id": "cand_fp_mobility_vs_collateral_bundle",
      "agenomeId": "ag_first_principles",
      "title": "Americans own collateral stacks, not trips",
      "summary": "The stated problem treats car use as the behavior to explain, but the durable pattern is a bundled balance-sheet object: secured debt, insurable collateral, registrable asset, and spatial anchor. Autonomy vendors optimize per-mile trip cost; households optimize monthly obligation, equity extraction, and life-layout fit. Until those layers decouple, cheaper rides do not unwind ownership.",
      "mechanism": "Separate demand (mobility minutes), incentives (loan approval, insurance pricing, trade-in cycles), constraints (parking geometry, commute topology, credit access), and feedback loops (depreciation → underwater loans → longer terms → higher payments → refusal to exit). Trip substitution via robotaxi only attacks the demand layer; finance, insurance, registration, and garage-adjacent lifestyle remain on the collateral loop.",
      "claimedDelta": "Reframes autonomy pressure from a technology race to a collateral-unbundling problem: the real cause of persistent ownership is the integrated capital-and-compliance bundle, not preference for driving.",
      "citedKnowledge": [
        "web-americans-are-refusing-to-make-their-ins",
        "web-tesla-s-robotaxi-rollout-remains-limited"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "cand_bs_ownership_maintenance_economy",
      "agenomeId": "ag_blindside",
      "title": "The neglected owner is the car-dependency industry",
      "summary": "Invert the consumer-centric frame: Americans keep financing and insuring cars because a distributed ownership-maintenance economy—dealers, lenders, insurers, municipalities, repair networks, parking landlords—prices and regulates life around one car nearby. Tesla-scale autonomy threatens that rent chain more than it threatens the driver habit.",
      "mechanism": "Search for the stakeholder whose revenue disappears if ownership thins. Registration fees, property-tax assumptions on garages, dealer F&I spreads, insurer float, and body-shop throughput all assume VIN permanence. Robotaxi at 59 vehicles is small, but OEM telematics plus direct settlement pilots signal platform capture of claims and lifecycle data—disintermediating adjusters first, then weakening the insurer-dealer adjacency that sustains the ownership ritual.",
      "claimedDelta": "Real cause shifts from cultural car love to institutional lock-in: ownership persists because adjacent institutions still earn on every financed, insured, parked, and repaired VIN.",
      "citedKnowledge": [
        "adjuster-disintermediation",
        "web-tesla-s-robotaxi-rollout-remains-limited"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "cand_bo_datacenter_capex_to_opex",
      "agenomeId": "ag_breakout",
      "title": "Cars follow the datacenter capex-to-opex fracture",
      "summary": "Map the case onto enterprise infrastructure: organizations kept buying servers long after cloud proved cheaper because capex, depreciation schedules, and vendor maintenance contracts lagged the new unit economics. American car life exhibits the same fracture—mobility opex is arriving while household capex (loan, garage, insurance stack) remains legally and spatially installed.",
      "mechanism": "Shared pressure geometry: (1) high upfront asset, (2) multi-year depreciation contract, (3) specialized maintenance priesthood, (4) switching cost embedded in physical footprint. Supervised FSD and limited robotaxi mirror early cloud—usable in pockets, not yet credible as full substitute. The lagging variable is not technology capability but contract and footprint unwinding.",
      "claimedDelta": "Predicts ownership decay as a staged migration—finance and parking commitments outlive functional trip substitution—rather than a sudden autonomy flip.",
      "citedKnowledge": [
        "web-tesla-s-robotaxi-rollout-remains-limited",
        "web-tesla-s-robotaxi-service-is-experiencing"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "cand_bt_vin_locked_mobility_meter",
      "agenomeId": "ag_breakthrough",
      "title": "One subscription retires five ownership chores",
      "summary": "A small mechanism—VIN- or account-locked mobility subscription that rolls payment, insurance, registration, maintenance, and trade-in into a single usage meter—could collapse the behavioral surface area that makes car ownership feel mandatory even when trips are partially automatable.",
      "mechanism": "High-leverage intervention targets the paperwork bundle, not the powertrain. When the platform owns depreciation risk and bills per mile/month, the household sheds insure-register-repair-trade-in decisions without waiting for full robotaxi density. FSD supervised mode becomes retention feature inside the bundle; robotaxi becomes overflow capacity. Payment refusal becomes the forcing function that makes bundling commercially attractive.",
      "claimedDelta": "Ownership unwinds when a single contract substitutes the chore cluster; autonomy accelerates the bundle only if it is sold as subscription-native, not as an owned-vehicle feature alone.",
      "citedKnowledge": [
        "web-americans-are-refusing-to-make-their-ins",
        "adjuster-disintermediation"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "cand_ci_seven_year_parking_lock",
      "agenomeId": "ag_constraint_injection",
      "title": "Seven-year loans meet immovable parking",
      "summary": "Name the binding constraint before any autonomy forecast: amortization length plus residential parking geometry. Americans organize daily life around a car nearby because the driveway, street permit, garage, and commute graph were chosen under ownership assumptions that outlast any robotaxi headline.",
      "mechanism": "Binding constraint = simultaneous (a) secured multi-year debt on a depreciating asset and (b) fixed spatial slot for that asset. Robotaxi reduces marginal trip cost but does not free the parking slot or erase negative equity. Until loan term, housing location, or parking policy changes, households keep insuring and registering a car they barely drive—payment stress is the visible symptom; spatial-financial lock is the cause.",
      "claimedDelta": "Explains why limited robotaxi rollout and operational friction barely dent ownership: the constraint is balance-sheet and land-use, not ride availability.",
      "citedKnowledge": [
        "web-americans-are-refusing-to-make-their-ins",
        "web-tesla-s-robotaxi-rollout-remains-limited"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "cand_pm_secured_lifestyle_anchor",
      "agenomeId": "ag_polymath",
      "title": "The car is a mobile mortgaged lifestyle anchor",
      "summary": "Fuse only after naming shared causal structure with homeownership: illiquid collateral tied to daily routine, social status, and emergency liquidity. Americans do not merely drive; they anchor time, credit, and identity to a titled asset that must stay insured, registered, and within reach.",
      "mechanism": "Shared structure across domains: collateralized commitment → sunk spatial choice → insurance/regulatory compliance → identity signaling → inability to exit without simultaneous lifestyle rewrite. Autonomy platforms attack trip execution; they do not automatically replace equity access (refi against car), teen mobility independence, or suburban distance budgets. Supervised FSD extends anchor life; it does not dissolve the secured-lifestyle loop.",
      "claimedDelta": "Real cause is the secured-lifestyle anchor, not transportation preference—explaining why financing and insuring persist even as utilization could fall.",
      "citedKnowledge": [
        "web-americans-are-refusing-to-make-their-ins",
        "web-tesla-s-robotaxi-service-is-experiencing"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "cand_sk_payment_stress_decoupled_from_autonomy",
      "agenomeId": "ag_skeptic",
      "title": "Payment mutiny is not an autonomy vote",
      "summary": "Start from the most likely error: treating payment refusal and Tesla autonomy as one causal story. Americans resisting insane car payments reflects credit-cycle stress and loan-term bloat; 59 robotaxis in three Texas cities and troubled early operations do not yet offer a reliable substitute for the owned-car chore bundle.",
      "mechanism": "Failure mode for the autonomy-disruption narrative: conflate affordability crisis with mode shift. Supervised FSD still requires an attentive driver in owned vehicles—reinforcing ownership. Robotaxi remains patchy and operationally brittle, so households cannot offload finance-insure-park-register chains at scale. The real near-term cause of ownership stress is payment arithmetic; the real near-term cause of ownership persistence is absent credible substitute for the whole bundle.",
      "claimedDelta": "Splits two phenomena—credit pain versus platform shift—preventing false confidence that robotaxi headlines explain why Americans still finance, insure, and garage cars.",
      "citedKnowledge": [
        "web-americans-are-refusing-to-make-their-ins",
        "web-tesla-s-robotaxi-rollout-remains-limited",
        "web-tesla-s-robotaxi-service-is-experiencing"
      ]
    }
  ]
